Yet, still much growth happen marriage NFL advanced metrics. example, denying sabermetrics movement drastically “altered baseball’s DNA” Heifetz (2019)]. Moreover, explained Seth Partnow’s outstanding Midrange Theory: Basketball’s Evolution Age Analytics, analytics movement NBA essentially killed midrange shot (briefly: beneficial try work ball directly basket (high-percentage shot) take 3-pointer, possible additional point statistically worth despite lower success probability opposed 2-point midrange shot) well traditional, “old-school” center position.Compared NBA MLB, NFL playing catchup analytics driving changes equivalent death midrange shot plethroa additional tactics changes baseball sabermetrics. Joe Banner, served President Eagles 2001-2012 Chief Executive Officer Browns 2012-2013, explained hesitation incorporate analytics NFL game planning result game “much driven conventional wisdom extreme degree” (Fortier 2020). Perhaps nobody encapsulates better Pittsburgh Steelers Head Coach Mike Tomlin. asked position analytics 2015 season, Tomlin explained:think ’s play game. can take analytics baseball things like football always going football. got lot respect analytics numbers, ’m going make judgements based numbers. game game. ’s emotional one played emotional driven men. ’s element game can’t measure. Often times decisions weigh heavily equation (Kozora 2015).Given Tomlin’s quote 2015, perhaps Steelers pivoted since now analytically inclined. seem case. poll NFL analytics staffers conducted ESPN, Steelers voted one least analytically advanced teams league.large gap least analytically inclined teams (Washington, Tenessee, Cincinnati, New York Giants, Pittsburgh) voted analytically inclined (Baltimore, Cleveland, Philadelphia, Houston). Just pure genius.’ ’s one .”article Washington Post, Sam Fortier argues many teams became inspired intimately introduce analytics game planning -field decisions 2017 season. run becoming Super Bowl Champions, Philadelphia Eagles aggressive 4th , going 26 times season converting 17 conversion percentage 65.4%. quick examination visualization data highlights absolutely staggering increase 4th aggressiveness among NFL head coaches 2017-2021:

96.3% increase number 4th attempts just 2017 2021. fact, numbers may actually higher quite conservative building plot considering 4th attempts took place offenseive team 5--95% winning probability prior two-minute warning either half. Even conservative limitations, increase staggering. numbers, however, support aggression. week one 2020 2021 season, going 4th “cost teams cumulative 170 percentage points win probability” (Bushnell 2021).Ben Baldwin, using nfl4th package part nflverse, tracked shift NFL coaching mentality regarding 4th decisions comparing 2014’s “go percentage” 2020. compared 2014 season, NFL coaches now much agreement analytics “go ” 4th relation expected gain win probability.
Figure 0.2: Credit: Ben Baldwin
surprising , considering Mike Tomlin’s quote NFL analytics staffers voting Steelers one least analytically driven temas league, Pittsburgh lost win probability either kicking punting “go ” situations 2020 NFL season. end, Ravens Browns - two teams voted analytically inclined - two best organizations knowing “go ” 4th based win probablity added. seems defined relationship teams buying analytics :

NFL’s turn towards aggressive 4th-decisions just one many analytics-driven changes occuring league. Another significant example Defense-Adjusted Value Average (DVOA), formula created Aaron Schatz, now editor chief Football Outsiders, sought challenge notion teams , first, establish running game order open passing game. changes apparent televisions screens Sunday afternoons Fall, others occuring behing scenes (analytics departments working scouting draft preparation, example). Indeed, use analytics NFL tightly engrained see prominent leagues. must remember certainly continued hold outs among NFL coaches (like Mike Tomlin).Despite coaching hold outs fully embracing analytics, “thirst knowledge football excessive sport desire get wins per dollar just high.” pipeline data continues grow, internally league data becomes publically accessible, “smart teams continue leave rock unturned push limits far data can take .” Joe Banner explained NFL long league coaches saying “well, way ’ve always done ,” league ripe major shift (Bechtold 2021).Banner’s belief teams searching every competitive advantage “leave rock unturned” driving force behind book. intents purposes, age analytics NFL stil infancy. Turning back, , 2017 season, Eagles’ management praised credited team’s analytics department part reason able win Super Bowl LII. Danney Heifetz argues, “changed language football.” NFL, explains, “copycat league” , witnessed increase 4th aggressivenes since 2017, teams immediately began carbon copy Philadelphia’s approach folding traditional football strategy new age analytics approach. modernity relationship long-held football dogmas analytics, nobody can quite sure impacts create gamemanship football.However, Heifetz opins, NBA MLB can serve roadmap analytics take NFL. Importantly, NFL’s relationship analytics still “first frontier likely sweeping change next two decades.” , sure next major impact analytics make, may occur. , ever-growing amount publicly accessible data, matter time discovered. example, interview Heifetz, Brian Burke - one forefather’s NFL analytics now writer ESPN - expressed belief next major impact “quantifying often quarterbacks make correct decision field.”seems every new NFL season results amateur analyst bringing groundbreaking model /approach table. Unlike, example, MLB little left discover terms sabermetrics new approaches understanding game associated strategy, NFL - lack better phrase - open playing field. data becoming avaliable public, now easier ever investigate ideas suspicions create models confirm intuition.example, originally greater Pittsburgh area big Steelers fan (certainly explains Steelers-centric examples use writing book). adamant belief Pittsburgh’s TJ Watt win 2021 Defensive Player Year award, despite many others calling Los Angeles’ Aaron Donald claim title. effort prove point, sought design coined Adjusted Defensive Impact. begin, wanted explore idea defensive sacks created equal, player’s true impact always perfectly represented top-level statistics.account , opted adjust add statistical weight sack statistics. done multiple areas. instance, players competed 17 regular-season games 2021. adjust , took total game played data (2,936) divided 17 (full season) achieve weighted adjustment 0.0058. TJ Watt played just 15 games 2021. adjusted equation, therefore, (17-‘games’) * 0.0058. result? gets bit credit adjustment , say, Myles Garrett played 17 regular-season games.Going model, created weighted adjustment solo sacks (0.90), negative weighted adjustment (-0.14) sack charted “unblocked,” weighted adjustment account many times player actually rushed QB compared many defensive snapes played. Using data SIS Data Hub, full code :end result? Taking account adjusted defensive impact, TJ Watt absolutey dominant 2021 season:
Figure 0.4: Adjusted Defensive Impact
examples - Ben Baldwin’s 4th-model, Football Outsiders’ DVOA, attempt quantify defensive player impact - just leading edge burgeoning analytics movement NFL. Moreover, beauty analytics mathamatician statistics buff order enter fray. takes genuine curiosity explore Bob Carroll, Pete Palmer, John Thorn coined “Hidden Game Football” desire learn, already, R programming language.
code:
options(digits = 2)

## selecting just information I want and then renaming
pass.data <- pass_rush_data %>%
  select(Player, Team, Games, `Pass Snaps`, `Pass Rushes`,
         `Solo Sacks`, `Ast. Sacks`, `Comb. Sacks`, 
         `Unblocked Sacks`, Hurries, Hits) %>%
  rename(total.snaps = `Pass Snaps`,
         total.rushes = `Pass Rushes`,
         solo.sacks = `Solo Sacks`,
         asst.sacks = `Ast. Sacks`,
         comb.sacks = `Comb. Sacks`,
         unblocked.sacks = `Unblocked Sacks`,
         player = Player,
         team = Team,
         games = Games,
         hurries = Hurries,
         hits = Hits)

## creating a new column to get percentages of snaps where player rushed
pass.data$rush.percent <- pass.data$total.rushes / pass.data$total.snaps

## getting weights to add to the equation
## for example, a solo sack is "more impressive" than an assisted sack. 
## to account for this, we will take the sum of combined sacks (995) and divide 
## by the sum of solo sacks (892) which is (0.9). Therefore, a weight of 0.9 will
## be placed on solo sacks.

## as well, a negative weight will be applied to unblocked sacks. Using the total
## of solo sacks (892) and dividing by the sum of unblocked sacks (128) gives us a
## NEGATIVE weight of 0.14 to be applied to unblocked sacks

## further, a weight must be applied to those players who played less than a complete season.
## the weight here works out to be 0.0058.

## summarizing information
calculated.impact <- pass.data %>%
  group_by(player) %>%
  summarize(
    adjusted.games = (17 - games) * 0.0058,
    adjusted.solo = solo.sacks * 0.9,
    adjusted.unblocked = unblocked.sacks / -0.14,
    adjusted.rush.percent = 0.81 - rush.percent,
    combined.impact = sum(adjusted.games + (solo.sacks * 0.9) + (unblocked.sacks * -0.14) + adjusted.rush.percent))

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language (*rough draft)
heading: 1.1 nflreadr: An Introduction to the Data
text: important part nflverse , course, data. begin, examine core data underpins nflverse: player weekly stats advanced robust play-–play data. Using nflreadr, end user able collect weekly top-level stats via load_player_stats() function much robust play--play numbers using load_pbp() function.may imagine, important distinction load_player_stats() load_pbp(). mentioned, load_player_stats() provide weekly, pre-calculated statistics either offense kicking. Conversely, load_pbp() provide 350 metrics every single play every single game dating back 1999.load_player_stats() function includes following offensive information:well, switching stat_type “kicking” provides following information:data returned robust play--play data covering next, load_player_stats() function extremely helpul need quickly (correctly!) recreate official stats listed either NFL’s website Pro Football Reference.example, let’s say need get Ben Roethlisberger’s total passing yard attempts 2021 season. via load_pbp() , need context, using load_player_stats() much efficient.
code:
offensive.stats <- nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021)
ls(offensive.stats)##  [1] "air_yards_share"             "attempts"                   
##  [3] "carries"                     "completions"                
##  [5] "dakota"                      "fantasy_points"             
##  [7] "fantasy_points_ppr"          "interceptions"              
##  [9] "pacr"                        "passing_2pt_conversions"    
## [11] "passing_air_yards"           "passing_epa"                
## [13] "passing_first_downs"         "passing_tds"                
## [15] "passing_yards"               "passing_yards_after_catch"  
## [17] "player_id"                   "player_name"                
## [19] "racr"                        "receiving_2pt_conversions"  
## [21] "receiving_air_yards"         "receiving_epa"              
## [23] "receiving_first_downs"       "receiving_fumbles"          
## [25] "receiving_fumbles_lost"      "receiving_tds"              
## [27] "receiving_yards"             "receiving_yards_after_catch"
## [29] "recent_team"                 "receptions"                 
## [31] "rushing_2pt_conversions"     "rushing_epa"                
## [33] "rushing_first_downs"         "rushing_fumbles"            
## [35] "rushing_fumbles_lost"        "rushing_tds"                
## [37] "rushing_yards"               "sack_fumbles"               
## [39] "sack_fumbles_lost"           "sack_yards"                 
## [41] "sacks"                       "season"                     
## [43] "season_type"                 "special_teams_tds"          
## [45] "target_share"                "targets"                    
## [47] "week"                        "wopr"
kicking.stats <- nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021, stat_type = "kicking")
ls(kicking.stats)##  [1] "fg_att"              "fg_blocked"          "fg_blocked_distance"
##  [4] "fg_blocked_list"     "fg_long"             "fg_made"            
##  [7] "fg_made_0_19"        "fg_made_20_29"       "fg_made_30_39"      
## [10] "fg_made_40_49"       "fg_made_50_59"       "fg_made_60_"        
## [13] "fg_made_distance"    "fg_made_list"        "fg_missed"          
## [16] "fg_missed_0_19"      "fg_missed_20_29"     "fg_missed_30_39"    
## [19] "fg_missed_40_49"     "fg_missed_50_59"     "fg_missed_60_"      
## [22] "fg_missed_distance"  "fg_missed_list"      "fg_pct"             
## [25] "gwfg_att"            "gwfg_blocked"        "gwfg_distance"      
## [28] "gwfg_made"           "gwfg_missed"         "pat_att"            
## [31] "pat_blocked"         "pat_made"            "pat_missed"         
## [34] "pat_pct"             "player_id"           "player_name"        
## [37] "season"              "season_type"         "team"               
## [40] "week"

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language (*rough draft)
heading: 1.1.1 Getting Weekly Player Stats via load_player_stats()
text: familar R, might seem logical following get Roethlisberger’s total passing yards number attempts 2021 season:can see ben.weekly output, matched official 2021 regular stats perfectly 3,740 passing yards 605 attempts. code just created several things. First, using nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021) place data R environment DF titled weekly.data.Next, group data together alike player_id (every individual player unique ID number) well player’s actual name. filtering level, looking just regular season (REG) within season_type also removing quarterbacks except Ben Roethlisberger. important note player names just first initial last name, without space period.filtering regular season, able summarize weekly data combined statistics summing weekly totals passing yards attempts.Unfortunately, still done. order get Roethlisberger’s name attached , needed , needed complete left_join ben.weekly rosters dataframes. , matching player_id gsis_id within roster information. information combined, able pull just Roethlisberger’s information select columns want.However, filtering player_name can lead signifcant issues results. excellent example Josh Allen. Let’s recreate code successfully provided Roethlisberger’s stats, replace Ben Josh Allen:output tells us Allen threw 4,049 yards 603 attempts 2021 regular season. check Pro Football Reference page tells us numbers incorrect. fact, 4,407 passing yards 646 attempts. end 358 passing yards 43 attempts short?answer comes Aaron Schatz, creator Football Outsiders, explained Tweet official Buffalo Bills’ scorer, week 3 NFL season, decided refer Allen “Jos.Allen” result Washington Commanders player named “Jonathan Allen.”double check , can run code , remove player_name filter switch searching just players Buffalo Bills using recent_team.Grouping player_id player_name (well filtering Buffalo), can see , indeed, Josh Allen data twice player_id. Moreover, math, can see numbers two entries add official statistics Pro Football Reference page.
code:
weekly.data <- nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021)

ben.weekly <- weekly.data %>%
  group_by(player_id, player_name) %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG" & player_name == "B.Roethlisberger") %>%
  summarize(total.yards = sum(passing_yards),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts))## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'player_id'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.
tibble(ben.weekly)## # A tibble: 1 x 4
##   player_id  player_name      total.yards n.attempts
##   <chr>      <chr>                  <dbl>      <int>
## 1 00-0022924 B.Roethlisberger        3740        605
josh.allen <- weekly.data %>%
  group_by(player_name) %>%
  filter(player_name == "J.Allen" & season_type == "REG") %>%
  summarize(total.yards = sum(passing_yards),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts))

tibble(josh.allen)## # A tibble: 1 x 3
##   player_name total.yards n.attempts
##   <chr>             <dbl>      <int>
## 1 J.Allen            4049        603
two.josh.allens <- weekly.data %>%
  group_by(player_id, player_name) %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG" & recent_team == "BUF") %>%
  summarize(total.yards = sum(passing_yards),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts))## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'player_id'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.
tibble(two.josh.allens)## # A tibble: 17 x 4
##    player_id  player_name  total.yards n.attempts
##    <chr>      <chr>              <dbl>      <int>
##  1 00-0027685 E.Sanders              0          0
##  2 00-0029000 C.Beasley              0          1
##  3 00-0031588 S.Diggs                0          0
##  4 00-0031787 J.Kumerow              0          0
##  5 00-0033308 M.Breida               0          0
##  6 00-0033466 I.McKenzie             0          0
##  7 00-0033550 D.Webb                 0          0
##  8 00-0033869 M.Trubisky            43          8
##  9 00-0033904 D.Dawkins              0          0
## 10 00-0034857 J.Allen             4049        603
## 11 00-0034857 Jos.Allen            358         43
## 12 00-0035250 D.Singletary           0          0
## 13 00-0035308 T.Sweeney              0          0
## 14 00-0035689 D.Knox                 0          0
## 15 00-0036187 R.Gilliam              0          0
## 16 00-0036196 G.Davis                0          0
## 17 00-0036251 Z.Moss                 0          0

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language (*rough draft)
heading: 1.1.1.1 Using load_player_stats() Correctly
text: avoid situations, load NFL rosters via nflreadr::load_rosters() function, require unnecessary code order merge two DFs together. Instead, can :efficient way gather correct player statistics group_by player_id , despite variation name, player_id remained Josh Allen. order include correct name output, can gather within summarize prior calcuating sum passing_yards attempts. , desire see Josh Allen’s number, can filter just name.
code:
josh.allen <- weekly.data %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG") %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>%
  summarize(player_name = first(player_name),
            total.yards = sum(passing_yards),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts)) %>%
  filter(player_name == "J.Allen")

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language (*rough draft)
heading: 1.1.2.1 An Example: 2021 QB Air Yards per Attempt Leaders
text: example, using group_by combine desired statistics based unique player_id , , avoid issues player names within data. filtering include just statistics regular season, first use summarize function grab first player_name associated player_id. , find two items: (1.) total number passing attempts QB outputted new row titled n.attempts regular season total QB’s air yards, outputted new row titled n.airyards.important note final row created summarize function statistic included within load_player_stats(). order find QB’s average air yards per attempt, must use first two items ’ve created simple division (created n.airyards divided n.attempts).Finally, “clear noise” QBs minimal attempts season, included filter include passers least 400 attempts. , arrange new DF sorting QBs descending order average air yards per attempt.end results look like :Russell Wilson led NFL 2021 9.89 air yards per attempt.
code:
data <- nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021)

ay.per.attempt <- data %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG") %>%
  summarize(player_name = first(player_name),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts),
            n.airyards = sum(passing_air_yards),
            ay.attempt = n.airyards / n.attempts) %>%
  filter(n.attempts >= 400) %>%
  select(player_name, ay.attempt) %>%
  arrange(-ay.attempt)
tibble(ay.per.attempt)## # A tibble: 25 x 2
##    player_name   ay.attempt
##    <chr>              <dbl>
##  1 R.Wilson            9.89
##  2 J.Hurts             8.99
##  3 B.Mayfield          8.73
##  4 M.Stafford          8.48
##  5 J.Allen             8.20
##  6 K.Cousins           8.16
##  7 J.Burrow            8.12
##  8 D.Carr              8.12
##  9 T.Brady             8.10
## 10 T.Bridgewater       8.04
## # ... with 15 more rows

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language (*rough draft)
heading: 1.2.1 An Example: QB Aggresiveness on 3rd Down
text: Sticking air yards example , let’s examine metric created using load_pbp() coined QB 3rd Aggressiveness. metric designed determine QBs NFL aggressive 3rd situations gauging often throw ball , pass, first line. interesting metric explore , just like many metrics NFL, air yards created equal. example, eight air yards 1st 10 less valuable eight air yards 3rd 5.First, let’s highlight code used create results metric break line--line.can see tibble() output results, Dak Prescott aggressive quarterback 3rd passing situations 2021 season, passing , beyond, line gain just 63% time.creating new dataframe called aggressiveness 2021 play--play originally collected using data <- nflreadr::load_pbp(2021), use group_by ensure data collected per individual quarterback via passer_id.However, couple items point clarify code. Moreover, certainly arguments made regarding “capture” scenarios data require “aggressiveness.”using group_by function lump data individual QB, use filter() function. course, want play_types “pass” 3rd downs. However, code, filtering just 3rd situations yards go five ten yards.personal decision end creating metric. logic? less five yards go 3rd , opposing defense able “sell ” pass question offense attempt gain first ground. Conversely, anything ten yards likely results defense selling pass, thus leaving imprint aggressiveness output quarterbacks.sake curiosity, can edit code include passing attempts 3rd 10 yards go first :results quite different first running metric, Dak Prescott now 4th aggressive QB, Kyler Murray moves top approaching nearly 70% aggressiveness rate 3rd . small change highlights important element analytics: much work result coder (ie., ) able justify decision-making process developing filters metric create.case, stand argument including just pass attempts 3rd 5 10 yards go accurate assessment aggressiveness , example, 3rd 8 yards go obvious passing situation cases.begs question, though: cases 3rd 8 yards go obvious passing situation?
code:
data <- nflreadr::load_pbp(2021)

aggressiveness <- data %>%
  group_by(passer_id) %>%
  filter(down == 3, play_type == "pass", ydstogo >= 5, ydstogo <= 10) %>%
  summarize(player_name = first(passer),
            team = first(posteam),
            total = n(),
            aggressive = sum(air_yards >= ydstogo, na.rm = TRUE),
            percentage = aggressive / total) %>%
  filter(total >= 50) %>%
  arrange(desc(percentage))

tibble(aggressiveness)## # A tibble: 30 x 6
##    passer_id  player_name  team  total aggressive percentage
##    <chr>      <chr>        <chr> <int>      <int>      <dbl>
##  1 00-0033077 D.Prescott   DAL      84         53      0.631
##  2 00-0035228 K.Murray     ARI      60         37      0.617
##  3 00-0036389 J.Hurts      PHI      65         40      0.615
##  4 00-0033873 P.Mahomes    KC       93         56      0.602
##  5 00-0034855 B.Mayfield   CLE      59         35      0.593
##  6 00-0036971 T.Lawrence   JAX      78         46      0.590
##  7 00-0036355 J.Herbert    LAC      87         51      0.586
##  8 00-0035710 D.Jones      NYG      60         35      0.583
##  9 00-0036212 T.Tagovailoa MIA      60         35      0.583
## 10 00-0026498 M.Stafford   LA       95         54      0.568
## # ... with 20 more rows
aggressiveness.under.10 <- data %>%
  group_by(passer_id) %>%
  filter(down == 3, play_type == "pass", ydstogo <= 10) %>%
  summarize(player_name = first(passer),
            team = first(posteam),
            total = n(),
            aggressive = sum(air_yards >= ydstogo, na.rm = TRUE),
            percentage = aggressive / total) %>%
  filter(total >= 50) %>%
  arrange(desc(percentage))

tibble(aggressiveness.under.10)## # A tibble: 33 x 6
##    passer_id  player_name  team  total aggressive percentage
##    <chr>      <chr>        <chr> <int>      <int>      <dbl>

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language (*rough draft)
heading: 1.2.1.1 QB Aggressiveness: Filtering for “Garbage Time?”
text: initial running QB Aggressiveness metric, Josh Allen 15th aggressive QB NFL 3rd 5 10 yards go. much success Buffalo Bills play 15th place ranking?Bills, conclusion 2021 season, largest positive point differential league 194 (Bills scored 483 points, allowing just 289). Perhaps Allen’s numbers skewed Bills often playing lead late game?account , can add information filter() function attempt remove referenced analytics community “garbage time stats.”Let’s add “garbage time” filter code ’ve already prepared:now using code, included three new items filter(). First, stipulating , aside distance inclusion, want plays occured offense’s win probability 5% 95%, well ensuring plays happen two-minute warning either half.decision range win probability numbers , , personal preference. nflfastR first released, analyst often used 20-80% range win probability. However, Sebastian Carl - one creators nflverse explained package’s Discord:Sebastian Carl: “generally conservative filtering plays using wp. Especially vegas wp model can reach >85% probs early game incorporates market lines. never understood 20% <= wp <= 80%”garbage time" filter. removing ton plays. general advice lower boundary something around 5% (.e., 5% <= wp <= 95%).Ben Baldwin followed Carl’s thoughts:Ben Baldwin: “agree . 20-80% used filter looking run-heavy team (outside range teams change behavior lot). possibly teams behave 4th downs. team player performance.”Based advice, typically stick 5-95% range filtering win probability using play--play data. , case, impact.mentioned, prior filtering garbage time, Allen 15th aggressive QB league nearly 52%. However, filtering garbage time, Allen rose 9th aggressive QB, slight increase percentage 54%.interesting example, though, Dak Prescott Cowboys. Dallas maintained second largest point differntial league (530 points 358 points , 172 point difference). Without garbage time filter, Prescott tops NFL aggressiveness rating 63%.adjusted garbage time? Prescott remained atop NFL aggressiveness rating 65.5%.Allen’s increase standings, Prescott remaining best league, specific metric, possible indicator inclusion “garbage time” filters provides slightly accurate result.
code:
aggressiveness.garbage <- data %>%
  group_by(passer_id) %>%
  filter(down == 3, play_type == "pass", ydstogo >= 5, ydstogo <= 10,
         wp > .05, wp < .95, half_seconds_remaining > 120) %>%
  summarize(player_name = first(passer),
            team = first(posteam),
            total = n(),
            aggressive = sum(air_yards >= ydstogo, na.rm = TRUE),
            percentage = aggressive / total) %>%
  filter(total >= 50) %>%
  arrange(desc(percentage))

tibble(aggressiveness.garbage)## # A tibble: 26 x 6
##    passer_id  player_name team  total aggressive percentage
##    <chr>      <chr>       <chr> <int>      <int>      <dbl>
##  1 00-0033077 D.Prescott  DAL      61         40      0.656
##  2 00-0036971 T.Lawrence  JAX      51         33      0.647
##  3 00-0035228 K.Murray    ARI      51         31      0.608
##  4 00-0026498 M.Stafford  LA       79         48      0.608
##  5 00-0033873 P.Mahomes   KC       68         41      0.603
##  6 00-0035710 D.Jones     NYG      50         30      0.6  
##  7 00-0036355 J.Herbert   LAC      67         38      0.567
##  8 00-0036972 M.Jones     NE       57         31      0.544
##  9 00-0034857 J.Allen     BUF      59         32      0.542
## 10 00-0029263 R.Wilson    SEA      56         30      0.536
## # ... with 16 more rows
